entry.title: How We Got Paid to Get Our PhD's While Raising Three Kids
entry.summary: (and you can too) [Chu News — Mark Chu / Education] First, let me briefly tell you about my personal journey to my PhD. Hopefully, it gives you an idea about what it takes, and what you might be able to do if you want…
entry.content:
(and you can too)

[Chu News — Mark Chu / Education] First, let me briefly tell you about my personal journey to my PhD. Hopefully, it gives you an idea about what it takes, and what you might be able to do if you want a PhD. with a limited budget too.

When I arrived in Hawaii for college, I had a small bank account and barely knew enough English to communicate. Before coming to America, one of my best friends questioned me on the phone, “Do you have enough money? Do you have enough language skills?”

“No, no.”

“That’s stupid. I predict, no, guarantee, you’ll come back in a year, top.”

“Well, I want to try,” I argued. 

“Yeah, good luck. You’ll need it. A lot.”

Four and a half years later, I graduated with honors and brought home a wife and two little babies. This crazy college business had a surprisingly good ending, and everyone thought that should be the end of it.

However, after staying in a job I hated for one plus year — I started fantasizing about smashing into the car in front of me— and also due to the nagging thought of “what if we could have a Master’s?” We both again applied to a small Master’s program in counseling in Kansas. On top of that, my wife gave birth to our third child.

Again, our decision sparked many pieces of advice like fire shooting at us: “Are you crazy!? You want to go back to America again with 3 little kids??” “And both of you going to school full time?!” “How much money do you have? What? $2,000!? I didn’t know your kids feed on dirt.”

“The school offers both of us Teaching Assistantships, which means we don’t have to pay a dime for tuition, and we get paid,” I said. Till today, I’m still surprised that so many students have no clue about this. But more about that later.

“That’s amazing! How did you know about this opportunity?” at least one asked, then added, “But you guys are still crazy. Who will take care of your kids when you both are going to school or working?”

“We’ll manage. You just watch,” I raised my head high.

Well, this time, we learned our lesson.

Because we were so damn poor, all we could afford was a run-down apartment. Whenever I put a pan on the stove, some roaches the size of a nickel would drop into it. Pretty soon they were everywhere, crawling on the floor and tables teasing us “you can’t catch me.” A month later, every single one of us started visiting the ER — more than once — for different reasons, from pneumonia to scarlet fever. Luckily, the hospital had a financial assistance program for people under the poverty line.

We struggled hard to balance everything: work, school, kids. Many times we had to beg our professors to bring at least one baby to the class. When it was time for our practicum, we found it was impossible to arrange, unless we locked our kids in a cage.

And then, another blow struck: I was kicked out of the funding because I didn’t look right in one professor’s eyes. Now, without my part of the income and my wife’s start of her practicum, the food stamp and tax return became our only lifeline. And when I questioned my possibility of pursuing my counseling goal, that professor was straightforward: “Not possible.”

That dream was not totally dead; I could go to another school. But if I needed to go to another school, I wanted a way more ambitious goal: a doctoral degree. I’ve proved many doubters wrong, now I needed to prove her wrong.

It was a very long shot. I’ve always been a cautious and fearful person, but for some reason, when it came to education, I became the biggest gambler.

We borrowed $6,500 from my wife’s friend (who was a saint with a heart of gold) for me to pay my last semester’s tuition to get a “General Psychology” Master’s— a useless degree. Then I carefully selected the programs I’d like to apply to. Long story short, I was accepted by Kansas State University's Social Psychology program.

But here came the problem: my wife was still in her practicum. If we moved now, she wouldn’t have her counseling degree. When I asked K-State if they could keep my position for next year, the answer was no. I’d have to apply again, and no guarantee of acceptance.

When you have a family, any of your life decisions must be for the benefit of the whole family; it’s NOT about you anymore. My family would be more secure if my wife got her degree. Therefore, I gave up my position and stayed home to care for the kids for a year.

A tough year went by. I applied again — and was accepted. But not only that, this time, I got the Teaching Assistant funding — which, again, meant a full tuition waiver and a yearly stipend — but because K-State was a way bigger school, the stipend was twice as much. The wait turned out to be the better outcome.

We moved to Manhattan, Kansas, a small and friendly town full of hope. I knew perfectly I wasn’t equipped to be a Ph.D. student but figured maybe I could fool the whole program even just a semester. A “partial completion of a doctoral degree” still sounded better than a master’s degree.

With our budget, we couldn’t find a place to rent for a family of 5. Finally we got a very small basement; a regular McDonald’s dining space would be at least three times as big. It was depressing to squeeze two adults and three children into that dungeon; we just had to fight to survive.

Then it was my wife’s turn to receive a blow from life: she couldn’t find a job. After 3 months, she fell into depression. She wouldn’t talk to me, wouldn’t engage in any activity, lay on the sofa and cried all night.

This couldn’t go on, I thought; she had to do something. Anything. “How about applying for a Ph.D. program here?” I suggested.

She did, and was accepted to the Family Study program. She also found a Research Assistant job. With both our incomes and the yearly tax return, we paid off our debt to my wife’s saint friend. Then we moved out of that dungeon to a townhouse. Both of us worked 20 hours per week, completed our assignments/research, and raised three kids. I got another Master’s and then a Ph.D. after 7 years; my wife got her Ph.D. one year after me. I sent out sixty applications for tenure-track faculty positions and got three offers — which was considered very lucky. We ended up at a small university in New Mexico. Right now we both are tenured professors.

That is our story.

So as you can see, throughout our time at K-State, we both got our Master's and PhDs without paying a dime — and got paid. Now you might think we lived like homeless people, or even eating grass and wearing rags. Not at all. Yes, we didn’t have furniture except for mattresses and a dining table. Our car was small without AC and with an overheating problem. But we ate normal food, wore normal clothes, managed to travel to other cities/states for entertainment every summer — and still saved some money. We didn’t live like a king, but comfortable enough.

If you’re dreaming about graduate schools, maybe even a doctoral degree, but worry about not being able to afford it, don’t be afraid. If I can do it, chances are SO CAN YOU. I’d like to share with you my knowledge and experience; hopefully they can be helpful to you.

Graduate school applications can be daunting and intimidating, and there’re many factors to consider. In this article, let’s simply focus on the financial part.

First, it’s my philosophy: try your best NOT to get into debt. I know student loans are popular, but before you’ve truly exhausted all the options, I strongly suggest you don’t consider that. When I was applying for Ph.D. programs, my thought was, “If they don’t give me enough money, I simply won’t go.” Getting into debt is like having bubble gum in your hair. True, we borrowed $$ from my wife’s friend, but it was HER who sensed our critical problems and volunteered, and she told us, “Don’t worry about when to pay it back, and no interest.” We managed to pay her off in about two years, but we owed her almost our lives. I highly doubt your loans will be half as forgiving and understanding like my wife’s friend.

Now, if no loans (at least for now), then what options do you have?

Before I tell you the secret, it goes without saying that you need to be a hard-working student (also, it’s a big plus if you have at least some research experience; we’ll talk about it in another article), because that maximizes your chance of getting paid in graduate schools.

The secret: when you apply for graduate schools, you have to find out every detail about their TEACHING/RESEARCH assistantships.

Once you decide on the programs you want to apply to, ask them about the teaching/research assistantships. This is how we got paid for our PhDs and raised a family. Graduate schools, especially bigger research-oriented schools, have more money and therefore more positions to hire graduate students to work. The positions mainly fall into these two categories.

A teaching assistant position requires you to help a professor such as grading, proctoring exams, and other tasks the professor sees fit. Usually, these positions are available in the department you’re applying to, though there might be some positions available in other departments sometimes (it’s rare though, cause people like to hire students within their own departments for the teaching position). The great things about this position are: first, in quite a few cases, it offers a full tuition waiver — you do NOT have to pay a dime. Even if it’s not a full waiver, you should only pay in-state tuition (that’s called a partial waiver), which is way, way cheaper than the full tuition. Second, it has a stipend (but usually only nine months; no job for the summer). Also, you get to see how professors teach and handle a class. Some even let you teach a class. Many schools (like K-State) even have a training program for graduate students to be full-time instructors (which means more extra cash). If you’re interested in teaching, this is a great opportunity. When I got my Ph.D. and started looking for a job, I already had about five years of teaching experience under my belt, which was a big help. Though because this is a position with potential, it might not be available for every student, and only lasts about three to five years. But if this year you didn’t get it, wait for the next year and apply again, you might get lucky.

A research assistant position is usually available everywhere on campus. It’s called “research assistant,” but sometimes it doesn’t involve a lot of research. Basically, whenever a program or a department wants to hire some student workers, they post the position and all students around campus are eligible to apply. The good things about this position are: first, it offers a partial tuition waiver, so you only have to pay in-state tuition. Second, the stipend can sometimes be up to twelve months, more than the teaching assistant. Finally, it’s more available than the teaching assistant position cause you can apply everywhere.

Now, let’s suppose you get either a TA or RA, congrats! Now you have to strive to keep this job. Work hard, kick ass, be helpful. This is not the time to fight with anyone unless you’re at the receiving end of prejudice/harassment. About three years into my TA, another more long-term teaching position was available, and because I was somewhat a popular instructor they thought of me. That was why I could support my family for seven years at K-State. And here I can’t express my sincere appreciation to every professor in the department enough. They’d helped me whenever I needed it.

Indeed, from my story, you can see that we were extremely lucky. For a very long time, we were way, way below the poverty line, and we struggled hard. But at every turn, some good things happened and we prevailed. However, you don’t have to struggle like we did, especially if you don’t have little kids. Plus, chances are English is your native language, you don’t have to worry about fighting to understand what others say, having serious self-doubt about whether you can teach in another language to the point of a panic attack. The first time I taught, it was an assignment from a professor, and his class had 150 students. I rehearsed the whole fifty-minute lecture, every single word I’d say, at least five times. When I got on the stage, my mouth was dry like fire, my heart was pounding in my ears. But then I thought of all the exciting things I wanted to tell them, and I opened my mouth.

Now I’m a tenured professor.

My best wishes to you. Feel free to ask any questions, and I will write more about preparation for graduate school applications every week. Thanks and hope all is well with you.

entry.title: Chu News
entry.summary: We Bring You the True News I created this blog to share with readers  Scientific findings, Social issues, My personal stories and/or observations, And a bit of my creative fiction writing. Except for the creative fiction, which will be noted, all the writings are based…
entry.content:
We Bring You the True News

I created this blog to share with readers 

Scientific findings,

Social issues,

My personal stories and/or observations,

And a bit of my creative fiction writing.

Except for the creative fiction, which will be noted, all the writings are based on scientific research and/or facts.  If they involve personal opinions, they will be specified and the readers can be the judge.

The purpose of heavily relying on science is not to say that religion and personal opinions are not important.  Plus, science is created by humans, and humans are not perfect.  However, the main issue is that science is much more reliable than personal beliefs. 

Why?  There are as many beliefs as the number of humans, and they all think they're right.  Just think, "This must be it and I know I'm right" is not going to make it a fact.  It's good to generate a hypothesis, but then one has to investigate and be willing to be proven wrong if that's the case, 

It's literally impossible to always be right, yet many see themselves that way.  The so-called "common sense" or "intuition" is as reliable as a career politician.

Some might question the validity of science and experts.  And indeed, they can make mistakes.  Actually, that is the point of science: To investigate and correct mistakes in order to find out the truth and facts.  That is why science heavily relies on the peer-review process.  

The fact that we enjoy cars, electricity, architecture, MRI machines, computers, artificial intelligence, etc., etc. is all because of science.  Like democracy, it's not perfect, but it's the best tool we have.

Therefore, in Chu News, we believe a basic understanding of science is critical in modern society.  And thus, we share.

entry.title: About Me
entry.summary: Hi all, My name is Mark Chu.  I'm originally from Taiwan, but now living in the US.  I'm married and have three children. I'm a professor in psychology at a tiny university in a remote Southwest town.  My field, specifically, social psychology, is a scientific…
entry.content:
Hi all,

My name is Mark Chu.  I'm originally from Taiwan, but now living in the US.  I'm married and have three children.

I'm a professor in psychology at a tiny university in a remote Southwest town.  My field, specifically, social psychology, is a scientific field studying human behavior in different situations.  My research focus is on sexism, but I'm also interested in adult attachment styles.

My interests include basketball, creative writing (fiction or nonfiction), drawing, and music, to name a few.

Thank you for visiting.

entry.title: The Edinburgh People I Met Cannot Be Nicer - Part 1 of 3
entry.summary: [Chu News — Mark Chu / Travel] Can we talk about the people in Edinburgh being so nice, you can't even begin to fathom what is going on? So this May (2026), my wife and I went to the UK for two weeks. It was…
entry.content:
[Chu News — Mark Chu / Travel] Can we talk about the people in Edinburgh being so nice, you can't even begin to fathom what is going on?

So this May (2026), my wife and I went to the UK for two weeks.  It was the first time we went abroad to a place other than Taiwan and America.  I chose London and Edinburgh, mostly because our oldest son went there a few years ago and couldn't stop talking about them.  I somewhat thought, "Well, if this trip isn't as good, at least we have someone else to blame."

We were a bit nervous: A place we weren't familiar with, what if we got lost?  Couldn't figure out how to pay whatever fees?  Had no means to pay?  You know, all the greenie, naive tourists' typical worries.

After a ten-hour flight with Virgin Atlantic, in which the on-flight entertainment didn't work at all, and they didn't have a regular phone charger plug-in (I guess European airlines are the same?) so I couldn't use my phone, we arrived.  

And lo and behold, right after getting out of the airport looking for The Tube, we got lost.  After aimlessly walking round and round, in and out of the Tube gates, arguing where the right places were, a couple was kind enough to notice our struggle and led us to the right way.  Not only that, they asked us where we were going and told us how to get there.  So now, Londoners made a good first impression on us.

When we walked on the street, the first things that welcomed us were cars driving on the left side and weather as stable as my mood.  This minute it was sunny, the next minute it started raining.  After a long walk dragging our suitcases, we found our hotel.  

(Looking out of the windows from our hotel stairways)

I think not only in the UK, but also in other European countries, things typically equipped by default in an American hotel room aren't found here.  For example, a mini fridge, or a microwave.  Also, there are no washer and dryer in the hotel.  No free breakfast.  I have to say the customer service was not bad though.  He started a small chat, and agreed to keep our suitcases in a storage room before check-in.

A friend of ours in London described the weather in that particular day, "Going through four seasons in one day."

We were sitting in a small Mediterranean restaurant looking at the storm in awe. The boss was outgoing and friendly, and the food was great.  We loved the lentil soup.  And the best yet was NO TIP required.  In America, the service isn't any better at all, and they demand 20% more.  

The next day, while I was walking and about to turn right, I heard the bell from a bike.  Without thinking, I kept walking, until a high schooler on a bike turning left was about to hit my face.  Right away I realized my mistake: I was walking on the right.  But the high schooler said, "Sorry," and rode away.  I believe I said sorry as well.  It was my fault, not his fault anyway, yet he apologized. 

Small things.  Though up to this point, I was convinced Londoners were just very polite and nice.

Many times while we walked around, we saw dogs without leashes.  But like their owners, they were either friendly or simply minding their own business.  By golly, are London dogs trained by the culture too?  Have gone to manners school?

My wife wanted to go to a park to see birds and took photographs.  This was the main purpose of the trip.  She found St James's Park that seemed to be worth a visit, so off we went.  But we had no idea what kind of place it was and what we were walking into.

My wife heard a lot of birds singing coming from the building on our right and said, "I need to find a way in." I saw all the sharp spikes on the top of the walls and said, "I don't think you can... this looks like a very important building."

There were many people from all over the world taking pictures, and at that moment we realized we had turned into tourists admiring this attraction.  What's funny is I've lived in America for about thirty years, and the first capitol building I've visited outside of Taiwan's government was the UK's.  No, I've never been to White House yet.

I guess he was in his 60s.  It was kind of chilly, but he wore light clothes and was barefoot.  And he was a bird whisperer.  Out of nowhere, he greeted us and started talking about the birds while feeding them.  He was knowledgeable, very sociable, and enthusiastic.  My wife got a few good bird pics. 

But suddenly we heard a scream, a teenage girl crying out loud.  Everyone turned to look at what was going on.  The girl was probably on the spectrum, and her toy or something important to her was dropped into a restricted area.  Her mom was comforting her.  I think they believed that the thing was lost forever.  A guard who was far away on the other side saw what happened, ran into the area, picked the thing up, gave it to the girl, and had a quick chat with them.  Everything was fine now.

I guess we indeed experienced a piece of the UK in the park.  We enjoyed it.

The next day, we visited Chiswick House and Gardens, right next to our hotel.  It was big, with many people jogging, walking dogs, or resting and chatting.  By now, readers should know that pretty much every garden in the UK is beautiful, but here are a few photos presented as evidence.

However, the garden, though pretty, had a smell of dogs' poop lingering everywhere.  Animals have the right to enjoy the place as much as we do, so we don't complain.

The garden has a restaurant, and right next to it is the public bathroom.  The men's room was ok, but my wife said there was neither tissue paper nor soap in the women's.  Bummer.

We have a friend in London.  He and his husband couldn't be nicer.  They brought us places and treated us to all the fancy meals.  They made sure we knew what the UK was like and our trip was worthwhile.

One of the best ways to learn about a culture, of course, is food.  They brought us to a traditional English breakfast place, Ace Cafe London.  The food was great.  Unfortunately, I didn't think to take a picture until halfway done...

We didn't know London had a lot of canals.  Some were like overpasses with boats roaming on them.  We were very amazed.

This is one canal.

 

 

It was in the afternoon, we were down on the street, and we noticed a huge crowd gathering.  They were shouting and singing.  "Football fans," our friend said.  There was a big game coming, and the whole area was packed with people, and I'm sure some (or many) with alcohol in their hands.  Sometimes they became so loud, you'd think a big fight would start any time.  But they were just very enthusiastic.

That's definitely a lovely cultural phenomenon I like to see.

Whether you know about the issues or not, whether you like it or not, I believe we agree that everyone must fully support basic human rights.  Basic.  Human. Rights.

 

The following day, our friends took us to the University of Cambridge.  I always wanted to go there; three of my favorite Brits all graduated from there -- David Attenborough, Hugh Laurie, and John Oliver (by last name alphabetical order).  It must be a great school.  By the way, Happy 100th Birthday, Sir Attenborough!

I didn't know, but the U of Cambridge is in the city of Cambridge--and there is not a "gate," "fence," or "wall" for the campus.  All the colleges, departments, buildings are spread around the city.  So we simply walked around to visit as many places as possible.

 

The next day we visited this vegetarian restaurant.  I vowed never to visit the same restaurant twice so we could try different food, good or bad.  This one was ok--and surprise, it asked for a tip.  I think I could have chosen "no tip;" after all, this is the UK, but I gave a little.  Bad habit.

The last one was arguably the best.  What do you think?

And this concluded our stay in London.  It's a great city.

Next, we head toward another great one, Edinburgh.
